description: The Emu is Australia's largest native bird and the second-tallest living bird in the world. These flightless birds roam vast distances across the Australian mainland, feeding on plants, insects, and small animals.
diet: Omnivore — seeds, fruits, flowers, insects, and small vertebrates
funFact: Emus can sprint at speeds up to 50 km/h and are excellent swimmers.

description: The Southern Cassowary is a large, flightless bird with a distinctive blue neck and a prominent casque on its head. Found in the tropical rainforests of Far North Queensland, it plays a vital role in seed dispersal.
diet: Frugivore — primarily fallen fruits, also fungi, snails, and small vertebrates
funFact: Cassowaries are considered the most dangerous bird in the world due to their powerful kick and dagger-like claws.

description: Famous for its unmistakable laughing call, the Laughing Kookaburra is the largest member of the kingfisher family. It is a beloved icon of the Australian bush, often seen perched on fence posts and branches.
diet: Carnivore — insects, lizards, snakes, mice, and small birds
funFact: Kookaburras are territorial and their laughing chorus at dawn and dusk marks their territory boundaries.

description: The Rainbow Lorikeet is one of Australia's most colourful birds, with bright plumage of blue, green, orange, and red. These energetic parrots are commonly seen in noisy flocks feeding on nectar and pollen in urban gardens and bushland.
diet: Nectarivore — nectar, pollen, fruits, seeds, and occasional insects
funFact: Rainbow Lorikeets have a brush-tipped tongue specially adapted for extracting nectar from flowers.

description: The Sulphur-crested Cockatoo is a large white parrot with a distinctive yellow crest that it raises when excited or alarmed. Highly intelligent and social, these birds are found across eastern and northern Australia.
diet: Herbivore — seeds, nuts, berries, flowers, roots, and grubs
funFact: These cockatoos can live over 80 years and have been observed teaching each other to open garbage bins.

description: The Galah is one of the most abundant and widespread cockatoos in Australia, easily recognised by its pink and grey plumage. Often seen in large, noisy flocks, it has adapted well to both rural and urban environments.
diet: Herbivore — seeds, grains, nuts, berries, and roots
funFact: In Australian slang, 'galah' is used to describe someone acting foolishly, owing to the bird's playful antics.

description: The Budgerigar is a small, nomadic parrot native to Australia's arid interior. Wild budgies are green and yellow with black barring, forming massive flocks that swirl like clouds over the outback.
diet: Granivore — grass seeds and cereal grains
funFact: Budgerigars are the third most popular pet in the world, after dogs and cats.

description: The Cockatiel is a small, graceful cockatoo with a distinctive pointed crest and orange cheek patches. Native to the Australian outback, it is a highly social bird found near water sources in arid regions.
diet: Granivore — seeds, grains, and fruits
funFact: Cockatiels are the smallest members of the cockatoo family and are the most popular pet bird in Australia.

description: The Australian King Parrot is a striking bird with vivid red and green plumage. Males have an entirely red head and breast, while females are green with a red belly. They inhabit dense rainforests and wet eucalyptus forests along the east coast.
diet: Herbivore — fruits, seeds, nuts, berries, and blossoms
funFact: King Parrots are one of the few parrots where males and females look completely different.

description: The Crimson Rosella is a medium-sized parrot with rich crimson plumage and bright blue cheeks and tail feathers. Found in forests and gardens of southeastern Australia, it is a common visitor to bird feeders.
diet: Herbivore — seeds, fruits, flowers, nectar, and insects
funFact: Juvenile Crimson Rosellas are mostly green and gradually moult into their red adult plumage over two years.

description: Major Mitchell's Cockatoo is considered one of the most beautiful cockatoos with its soft pink and white plumage and striking red and yellow banded crest. It inhabits arid and semi-arid woodlands across inland Australia.
diet: Herbivore — seeds, nuts, roots, and fruit
funFact: These cockatoos mate for life and can live over 75 years in captivity.

description: The Palm Cockatoo is a large, smoky-grey cockatoo with a massive bill and prominent red cheek patches. Found in the Cape York Peninsula, it is famous for using sticks to drum on hollow trees.
diet: Herbivore — pandanus nuts, seeds, and fruits
funFact: Palm Cockatoos are the only birds known to use tools as musical instruments, drumming sticks against trees.

description: Australia's largest bird of prey, the Wedge-tailed Eagle has a wingspan of up to 2.3 metres and a distinctive wedge-shaped tail. It soars effortlessly on thermal currents, scanning the landscape for prey.
diet: Carnivore — rabbits, hares, wallabies, reptiles, and carrion
funFact: Wedge-tailed Eagles have been recorded soaring at altitudes over 2,000 metres.

description: The Peregrine Falcon is the fastest animal on Earth, reaching speeds over 380 km/h during its hunting stoop. In Australia, it is found across diverse habitats from coastal cliffs to city skyscrapers.
diet: Carnivore — medium-sized birds, bats, and occasionally small mammals
funFact: Peregrine Falcons have a third eyelid to protect their eyes and special baffles in their nostrils to breathe during high-speed dives.

description: Australia's largest owl, the Powerful Owl has piercing yellow eyes and is a formidable nocturnal predator. It inhabits dense forests along the eastern seaboard, hunting possums and gliders at night.
diet: Carnivore — possums, gliders, flying foxes, and birds
funFact: Powerful Owls are known to carry their prey back to a favourite perch and hold it in their talons all day.

description: The Nankeen Kestrel is a small, graceful falcon known for its habit of hovering motionless in the wind while scanning the ground for prey. Its rufous-brown plumage and pointed wings make it easy to identify.
diet: Carnivore — insects, mice, lizards, and small birds
funFact: Nankeen Kestrels can see ultraviolet light, which helps them detect rodent urine trails that glow under UV.

description: The White-bellied Sea Eagle is a majestic raptor found along Australian coastlines, rivers, and large inland waterways. With its white head and belly contrasting dark grey wings, it is an iconic coastal predator.
diet: Carnivore — fish, sea snakes, turtles, waterbirds, and carrion
funFact: Mating pairs perform spectacular aerial courtship displays, locking talons and cartwheeling through the sky.

description: The Black Swan is an elegant waterbird unique to Australia, with striking black plumage and a bright red bill. It is the state bird of Western Australia and is found on lakes, rivers, and estuaries across the continent.
diet: Herbivore — aquatic plants, algae, and grasses
funFact: Before the discovery of Australia, Europeans believed all swans were white, making the Black Swan a symbol of the unexpected.

description: The Australian Pelican has the longest bill of any living bird, up to 50 cm long. These large waterbirds are found across Australia wherever there is water, from coastal shores to inland lakes and rivers.
diet: Piscivore — fish, crustaceans, and occasionally small waterbirds
funFact: Their bill pouch can hold up to 13 litres of water, which they use to scoop up fish.

description: The Brolga is a tall, elegant crane renowned for its intricate mating dance. Standing over a metre tall with a distinctive red head patch, it inhabits wetlands and grasslands across northern and eastern Australia.
diet: Omnivore — tubers, seeds, insects, molluscs, and frogs
funFact: The Brolga's elaborate dance, involving leaping, wing-stretching, and head-bobbing, has inspired Aboriginal Dreamtime stories.

description: The Royal Spoonbill is a large white wading bird with a distinctive black, spoon-shaped bill. It feeds by sweeping its bill side to side through shallow water to catch fish and crustaceans.
diet: Carnivore — fish, shrimp, crabs, and aquatic insects
funFact: During breeding season, Royal Spoonbills grow long white plumes on the back of their heads.

description: The Australian Wood Duck is a medium-sized duck with a distinctive maned appearance. Unlike most ducks, it grazes on grassland and is often seen far from water, perching in trees near farm dams.
diet: Herbivore — grasses, clover, and aquatic vegetation
funFact: Australian Wood Ducks nest in tree hollows, sometimes up to 10 metres above the ground, and their ducklings jump down to join their parents.

description: The Superb Fairy-wren is a tiny, energetic bird where breeding males display a brilliant iridescent blue plumage. These social birds live in family groups and are one of the most recognised small birds in Australia.
diet: Insectivore — insects, spiders, and small seeds
funFact: Male Superb Fairy-wrens pluck yellow petals to present to females as part of their courtship display.

description: The Australian Magpie is one of the most recognised and beloved birds in Australia, known for its melodious carolling song. Black and white plumaged, intelligent, and bold, it thrives in suburban areas across the country.
diet: Omnivore — insects, larvae, worms, snails, and small vertebrates
funFact: Magpies can recognise up to 100 individual human faces and remember those who have threatened them.

description: Also known as the Peewee or Mudlark, the Magpie-lark is a bold black and white bird commonly seen across Australia. It builds distinctive mud nests on horizontal branches and is known for its territorial displays.
diet: Insectivore — insects, larvae, earthworms, and freshwater invertebrates
funFact: Magpie-larks often attack their own reflection in windows, thinking it is a rival bird.

description: The Willie Wagtail is a small, fearless bird easily identified by its constant tail-wagging behaviour and black and white plumage. Despite its small size, it boldly defends its territory against much larger birds.
diet: Insectivore — flying insects, beetles, moths, and spiders
funFact: Willie Wagtails have been seen riding on the backs of cattle and horses to catch insects disturbed by their movement.

description: The Splendid Fairy-wren is a tiny bird where breeding males display an intense cobalt-blue plumage that is among the most vivid of any Australian bird. Found mainly in Western and South Australia in scrubby habitats.
diet: Insectivore — insects, spiders, and small seeds
funFact: Splendid Fairy-wrens are socially monogamous but genetically promiscuous, with most chicks fathered by males outside the pair.

description: The male Satin Bowerbird has glossy blue-black plumage with striking violet-blue eyes. Famous for building elaborate bowers decorated with blue objects to attract females, it is one of nature's most creative architects.
diet: Omnivore — fruits, flowers, leaves, seeds, and insects
funFact: Male bowerbirds collect blue objects including bottle caps, straws, and pegs to decorate their bowers and impress females.

description: The Blue-winged Kookaburra is the tropical cousin of the Laughing Kookaburra, with striking blue wing patches and a lighter, more maniacal call. Found across northern Australia in open woodlands and paperbarks.
diet: Carnivore — insects, lizards, frogs, fish, and crustaceans
funFact: Unlike Laughing Kookaburras, Blue-winged Kookaburras have a harsh, cackling call that sounds quite different.

description: The Sacred Kingfisher is a medium-sized kingfisher with turquoise-blue and buff plumage. Despite its name, it often hunts far from water, catching insects and small reptiles in open woodland.
diet: Carnivore — insects, crustaceans, lizards, and small fish
funFact: Sacred Kingfishers nest in termite mounds, tree hollows, and even earthen banks.

description: The Azure Kingfisher is a small, jewel-like bird with vivid blue upperparts and a rich orange breast. It perches low over freshwater streams and rivers, diving with remarkable speed to catch small fish.
diet: Piscivore — small fish, crustaceans, and aquatic insects
funFact: Azure Kingfishers can plunge-dive into water from a perch at speeds that would stun most small birds.

description: The Noisy Miner is an assertive grey honeyeater with a yellow bill, eye patch, and feet. Living in large communal groups, they aggressively mob larger birds and other species, dominating their territory.
diet: Omnivore — nectar, fruits, insects, and lerps
funFact: Noisy Miners are so aggressive that they can drive away all other bird species from an area, reducing local biodiversity.

description: The New Holland Honeyeater is a striking black and white bird with yellow wing patches. Active and vocal, it is one of the first birds described from Australia, collected during Captain Cook's voyage in 1770.
diet: Nectarivore — nectar, pollen, fruits, and insects
funFact: New Holland Honeyeaters were one of the first Australian bird species to be scientifically described by Europeans.

description: The Eastern Spinebill is a small, elegant honeyeater with a long, curved bill perfectly adapted for probing flowers. With its rufous, black, and white plumage, it resembles a tiny hummingbird as it hovers at flowers.
diet: Nectarivore — nectar from native flowers, insects, and spiders
funFact: Eastern Spinebills can hover like hummingbirds while feeding, a rare ability among Australian birds.

description: The Red Wattlebird is one of Australia's largest honeyeaters, with grey-brown plumage, prominent yellow belly patch, and distinctive red wattles on the neck. It has a loud, raucous call and is common in eucalyptus woodland.
diet: Omnivore — nectar, fruits, insects, and small vertebrates
funFact: Red Wattlebirds are important pollinators of native plants, especially banksia and eucalyptus species.

description: The Tawny Frogmouth is a master of camouflage, resembling a broken tree branch when roosting during the day. Often mistaken for an owl, this nocturnal bird has a wide, frog-like mouth used for snapping up insects.
diet: Insectivore — moths, beetles, spiders, worms, slugs, and small frogs
funFact: Tawny Frogmouths are frequently voted Australia's favourite bird in popular surveys.

description: The Superb Lyrebird is one of the world's greatest vocal mimics, able to perfectly imitate the calls of other birds, chainsaws, car alarms, and camera shutters. Males have an elaborate lyre-shaped tail used in courtship displays.
diet: Insectivore — insects, spiders, worms, and small crustaceans found by raking leaf litter
funFact: A lyrebird's mimicry is so accurate that it can fool even the species it is imitating.

description: The Australian Brush-turkey is a large, ground-dwelling bird with black plumage, a bare red head, and a bright yellow wattle. Males build massive mound nests of decomposing vegetation to incubate their eggs using the heat generated.
diet: Omnivore — seeds, fruits, insects, and fallen fruit
funFact: Brush-turkey mound nests can be up to 4 metres wide and 1.5 metres tall, weighing several tonnes.

description: The Eclectus Parrot is remarkable for its extreme sexual dimorphism — males are bright green while females are vivid red and purple. Found in the rainforests of Cape York Peninsula, it is one of Australia's most colourful parrots.
diet: Frugivore — fruits, flowers, nuts, seeds, and leaf buds
funFact: Male and female Eclectus Parrots look so different that they were once classified as separate species.

description: The Australian White Ibis, affectionately nicknamed the "bin chicken", is a large white wading bird with a distinctive curved black bill and bald black head. Once mainly found in wetlands, it has adapted remarkably to urban environments.
diet: Omnivore — insects, crustaceans, food scraps, and waste in urban areas
funFact: The Australian White Ibis has become an internet meme known as the "bin chicken" for its scavenging habits in city bins.
